reasoning: The 2-year Treasury yield baseline for April 13 is anchored at 3.96%, a sudden short-term spike from the early April low of ~3.67%, driven by immediate inflation fears and "reciprocal tariff" news. Prior to this, the 2-year yield had been steadily declining since its January 2025 level of ~4.28%. The prevailing macroeconomic expectations and the Fed rate path (with CME FedWatch showing a ~55% probability of a rate cut in May) heavily pressure short-term yields downward. Furthermore, reports indicate that the Trump administration seeks to keep bond yields below 4% as a policy pillar. Since 3.96% is a sharp local peak amidst a longer term downward trend since the start of the year, rapid mean reversion or continued declines make it relatively unlikely to increase in the shortest time frame (p=0.35 for April 20). As the forecast horizon extends into May, July, and October, expanding statistical variance due to geopolitical and inflation uncertainties balances against the expected rate-cut drift, so the probabilities appropriately widen and regress toward the uninformative prior of 0.5.
